Paper 2


SME Mark Scheme · Paper 2 Hard


1. Use the question-specific mark scheme together with the mark bands.
2. Whenever relevant, carry-over (OFR) marks must be awarded.
3. A candidate may be penalised once for rounding/unit errors across (b) subparts.

Question 1 · Argentina
1. (a) (i) Define inflation. [2]

  Level                                                                                   Marks

  0         The work does not meet a standard described by the descriptors below.         0

  1         Vague definition.                                                             1
            The idea that inflation is a rise in prices in an economy.

  2         Accurate definition.                                                          2
            An understanding that inflation is a sustained increase in the general
            (average) price level of goods and services in an economy over a given
            period of time.

1. (a) (ii) Define current account. [2]
  Level                                                                                   Marks

  0         The work does not meet a standard described by the descriptors below.         0

  1         Vague definition.                                                             1
            The idea that the current account records trade flows between countries.

  2         Accurate definition.                                                          2
            An understanding that the current account is a component of the balance of
            payments that records net trade in goods and services, net primary income
            and net secondary income (current transfers) between a country and the rest
            of the world.


                                                     1                                 SME Predicted Paper
                                                                               SME Mark Scheme P2 · Hard


1. (b) (i) Change in Argentina’s inflation rate between 2019 and 2023 (percentage points). [2]
Inflation 2019 = 53.8%; inflation 2023 = 143.0%.
Change = 143.0 − 53.8 [1]
Answer = +89.2 percentage points [1]

1. (b) (ii) Percentage depreciation of the peso 2018 to late 2023. [3]
Using ARS per US$: 2018 = 20; late 2023 = 800.
% depreciation = [(800 − 20) / 800] × 100 [1]
= 97.50% [1]
Answer = 97.50% depreciation of the peso (correct units required for [1]).
Alternative accepted working: the US dollar appreciated from 20 to 800 ARS, a 3,900% appreciation
(i.e. peso lost 97.5% of its value).

1. (c) AD/AS diagram · deficit financing causes demand-pull inflation. [4]

 Level                                                                                     Marks

 0         The work does not meet a standard described by the descriptors below.           0

 1         There is a correct diagram OR an accurate written response.                     1-2
           For an AD/AS diagram with AD shifting right OR an explanation that deficit
           financing increases money supply / government expenditure [1], raising AD
           and the price level [1].

 2         There is a correct diagram AND an accurate written response.                    3-4
           For a correct AD/AS diagram with AD shifting right AND an explanation that
           central-bank financed deficits raise money supply [1], causing demand-pull
           inflation [1].


                                                   2                                    SME Predicted Paper
                                                                             SME Mark Scheme P2 · Hard


1. (d) Exchange rate diagram · devaluation effect on current account. [4]

 Level                                                                                   Marks

 0        The work does not meet a standard described by the descriptors below.          0

 1        There is a correct diagram OR an accurate written response.                    1-2
          For an exchange rate diagram with the peso depreciating OR an explanation
          that a weaker peso makes exports cheaper and imports dearer [1], improving
          the current account (Marshall–Lerner permitting) [1].

 2        There is a correct diagram AND an accurate written response.                   3-4
          For a correct exchange rate diagram AND an explanation that devaluation
          raises export competitiveness and reduces imports [1], improving the current
          account if the Marshall–Lerner condition is satisfied [1].

1. (e) Export tax on soybeans · effect on producers. [4]

 Level                                                                                   Marks

 0        The work does not meet a standard described by the descriptors below.          0

 1        There is a correct diagram OR an accurate written response.                    1-2
          For a demand-and-supply diagram showing a fall in producer revenue/output
          OR an explanation that an export tax reduces the net price received by
          producers [1], lowering output and producer surplus [1].

 2        There is a correct diagram AND an accurate written response.                   3-4
          For a correct demand-and-supply diagram (export tax lowers the price
          received by producers, quantity falls, loss of producer surplus shown) AND
          an explanation that soybean farmers receive a lower net price [1] and supply
          less to the market [1].


                                                 3                                   SME Predicted Paper
                                                                                        SME Mark Scheme P2 · Hard


1. (f) AD/AS diagram · IMF fiscal consolidation. [4]

 Level                                                                                               Marks

 0           The work does not meet a standard described by the descriptors below.                   0

 1           There is a correct diagram OR an accurate written response.                             1-2
             For an AD/AS diagram with AD shifting left OR an explanation that cuts in
             government expenditure and higher taxes reduce AD [1], leading to lower
             output and employment [1].

 2           There is a correct diagram AND an accurate written response.                            3-4
             For a correct AD/AS diagram with AD shifting left AND an explanation that
             fiscal consolidation reduces C and G [1], lowering real GDP and raising
             unemployment in the short run [1].

1. (g) Evaluate shock-therapy stabilisation in Argentina. [15]
Answers may include:
      • Definitions of stabilisation, fiscal consolidation, devaluation, inflation targeting, dollarisation.
      • Theory/diagrams: AD/AS (tight fiscal and monetary policy shifting AD left), exchange rate
      diagram, discussion of Marshall–Lerner.
      • Arguments for: credibility; rapid inflation decline; restoration of access to capital markets;
      elimination of multiple-rate distortions (Text B, 4); removal of harmful subsidies; potential growth
      after adjustment.
      • Arguments against: severe short-run recession; sharp rise in poverty (already at 40.1%, Table
      1); political instability; regressive impact of subsidy removal; loss of independent monetary policy
      under dollarisation.
      • Alternatives: gradual orthodox adjustment; heterodox stabilisation (wage/price pacts); currency
      board; targeted income support during adjustment.
      • Synthesis / conclusion: a reasoned conclusion · often that shock therapy may be necessary
      given severity of crisis but must be accompanied by social safety nets and credible institutional
      reforms.

  Marks            Level descriptor

  0                The work does not meet a standard described by the descriptors below.

  1–3              The response indicates little understanding of the specific demands of the question.
                   Economic theory is stated but it is not relevant.
                   Economic terms are stated but they are not relevant.
                   The response contains no evidence of synthesis or evaluation.
                   The response contains no use of text/data.

  4–6              The response indicates some understanding of the specific demands of the
                   question.
                   Relevant economic theory is described.
                   Some relevant economic terms are included.
                   The response contains evidence of superficial synthesis or evaluation.
                   The response contains limited use of text/data.

7–9     The response indicates understanding of the specific demands of the question, but
        these demands are only partially addressed.
        Relevant economic theory is partly explained.
        Some relevant economic terms are used appropriately.
        Where appropriate, relevant diagram(s) are included.
        The response contains evidence of appropriate synthesis or evaluation but lacks
        balance.
        The response includes some relevant information from the text/data.

10–12   The specific demands of the question are understood and addressed.
        Relevant economic theory is explained.
        Relevant economic terms are used appropriately.
        Where appropriate, relevant diagram(s) are included and explained.
        The response contains evidence of appropriate synthesis or evaluation that is
        mostly balanced.
        The use of information from the text/data is generally appropriate, relevant, and
        applied correctly.

13–15   The specific demands of the question are understood and addressed.
        Relevant economic theory is fully explained.
        Relevant economic terms are used appropriately throughout the response.
        Where appropriate, relevant diagram(s) are included and fully explained.
        The response contains evidence of effective and balanced synthesis or evaluation.
        The use of information from the text/data is appropriate, relevant, and is used to
        formulate a reasoned argument supported by analysis/evaluation.

Question 2 · India
2. (a) (i) Define nominal GDP. [2]

  Level                                                                                  Marks

  0        The work does not meet a standard described by the descriptors below.         0

  1        Vague definition.                                                             1
           The idea that nominal GDP is the value of output in an economy.

  2        Accurate definition.                                                          2
           An understanding that nominal GDP is the total market value of all final
           goods and services produced in an economy over a given period, measured
           at current prices (not adjusted for inflation).

2. (a) (ii) Define protectionism. [2]

  Level                                                                                  Marks

  0        The work does not meet a standard described by the descriptors below.         0

  1        Vague definition.                                                             1
           The idea that protectionism limits imports into a country.

  2        Accurate definition.                                                          2
           An understanding that protectionism involves the use of government policies
           such as tariffs, quotas, subsidies and administrative barriers to shield
           domestic producers from foreign competition.


                                                   6                                 SME Predicted Paper
                                                                                   SME Mark Scheme P2 · Hard


2. (b) (i) India’s real GDP per capita 2022–23 (to nearest US$). [3]
Nominal GDP = US$3,700 bn; population = 1,420 million.
GDP per capita = 3,700,000,000,000 / 1,420,000,000 [1]
= $2,605.63 ≈ $2,606 per person [1]
Answer = US$2,606 (accept US$2,605–2,606 depending on rounding; correct units required for
[1]).

2. (b) (ii) Current account deficit as a percentage of nominal GDP. [2]
= (−67 / 3,700) × 100 [1]
Answer = −1.81% of GDP [1]

2. (c) AD/AS · higher global oil prices. [4]

 Level                                                                                         Marks

 0         The work does not meet a standard described by the descriptors below.               0

 1         There is a correct diagram OR an accurate written response.                          1-2
           For an AD/AS diagram with SRAS shifting left OR an explanation that higher
           oil prices raise production costs [1], causing a rise in the price level and fall in
           real output [1].

 2         There is a correct diagram AND an accurate written response.                        3-4
           For a correct AD/AS diagram (SRAS left, P up, Y down) AND an explanation
           that oil is a key input to production and transport [1], producing cost-push
           inflation and slower growth [1].

                                                                                 SME Mark Scheme P2 · Hard


2. (d) Tariff diagram · infant industry protection. [4]

  Level                                                                                         Marks

  0        The work does not meet a standard described by the descriptors below.                0

  1        There is a correct diagram OR an accurate written response.                     1-2
           For a tariff diagram showing world supply + tariff raising domestic price above
           world price OR an explanation that tariffs raise price, increase domestic
           output [1] and give infant industries time to achieve economies of scale [1].

  2        There is a correct diagram AND an accurate written response.                         3-4
           For a correct tariff diagram with world supply, world supply + tariff, domestic
           supply/demand, consumer-surplus loss, producer gain, tariff revenue and
           welfare loss clearly labelled AND an explanation that tariffs protect infant
           industries while they build competitiveness [1], with welfare cost [1].

2. (e) Exchange rate diagram · remittance inflows. [4]

  Level                                                                                         Marks

  0        The work does not meet a standard described by the descriptors below.                0

  1        There is a correct diagram OR an accurate written response.                          1-2
           For an exchange rate diagram with demand for INR shifting right
           (appreciation) OR an explanation that remittances generate demand for
           rupees [1], causing the rupee to appreciate [1].

  2        There is a correct diagram AND an accurate written response.                         3-4
           For a correct exchange rate diagram (price of rupee rising) AND an
           explanation that overseas workers convert foreign currency into INR [1],
           raising demand for rupees and appreciating the currency [1].

                                                                                      SME Mark Scheme P2 · Hard


2. (f) Negative consumption externality · solid-fuel cooking. [4]

 Level                                                                                                Marks

 0           The work does not meet a standard described by the descriptors below.                    0

 1           There is a correct diagram OR an accurate written response.                  1-2
             For a negative consumption externality diagram with MSB < MPB and
             welfare loss triangle OR an explanation that solid-fuel use imposes
             health/environmental costs [1] so government subsidises cleaner gas to raise
             consumption toward the social optimum [1].

 2           There is a correct diagram AND an accurate written response.                  3-4
             For a correct negative consumption externality diagram (MSB < MPB, welfare
             loss) AND an explanation that a subsidy on clean gas corrects the externality
             by shifting consumption toward Q* [1] and improving welfare [1].
Alternatively: a positive consumption externality diagram for cleaner cooking gas (MSB > MPB) with a
subsidy correcting under-consumption is also acceptable.

2. (g) Discuss India’s cautious approach to FTAs and long-term growth/development. [15]
Answers may include:
      • Definitions: free trade, trade creation, trade diversion, protectionism, infant industries.
      • Theory/diagrams: tariff diagrams, consumer and producer surplus, AD/AS, comparative
      advantage.
      • Benefits of caution: protection of infant industries (Text B, 3); safeguarding farmers and dairy
      producers (Text B, 2); preserving policy space; managing import surges.
      • Costs of caution: exclusion from regional value chains (RCEP, Text B, 2); higher prices for
      consumers; limited access to foreign inputs; reduced FDI in export sectors.
      • Evidence: India’s growth to $3.7 trn (Text A, 1); ‘Make in India’ initiative (Text A, 2);
      supply-chain diversification benefits (Text B, 4); trade deficit of $240 bn (Text B, 1) offset by
      services exports and remittances.
      • Synthesis / conclusion: reasoned judgement, e.g., selective FTAs combined with strong
      domestic reform may deliver better long-term growth than either blanket protection or full
      liberalisation.

  4–6             The response indicates some understanding of the specific demands of the
                  question.
                  Relevant economic theory is described.
                  Some relevant economic terms are included.
                  The response contains evidence of superficial synthesis or evaluation.
                  The response contains limited use of text/data.
